title: Cosmic Shockwave Reshaped a Newly Discovered “Bow and Arrow” Galaxy
gist: A citizen astronomer sifting through radio telescope data flagged a radio galaxy nicknamed RAD-BAARG (Radio Bow-And-Arrow Radio Galaxy), whose jets are strikingly asymmetric — one curving backward into an arc, the other twisting into a snake-like tail — forming a structure 1.8 million light-years across, about 18 times the size of the Milky Way.

insights.claim: The radio galaxy nicknamed RAD-BAARG (Radio Bow-And-Arrow Radio Galaxy) was first flagged by a citizen astronomer examining radio telescope data, not by a professional survey team.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Illustrates that publicly available radio-survey datasets still yield unclassified astrophysical structures findable by amateur analysts, not just professional teams.
insights.locator: 'A citizen astronomer examining radio telescope data recently flagged something unusual'

insights.claim: Most galaxies observed via radio telescope show fairly symmetric jets of ionized particles streaming from their supermassive black holes, but RAD-BAARG's two jets are lopsided: one curves backward into a massive arc, and the other twists into a snake-like tail.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: The asymmetry itself is the scientific puzzle — it deviates from the standard symmetric-jet model used to characterize radio galaxies.

insights.claim: RAD-BAARG's total radio structure spans 1.8 million light-years, making it nearly 18 times larger than the Milky Way.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of the anomaly, giving a concrete size benchmark for follow-up observational studies.
insights.locator: 'its cosmic structure is 1.8 million light-years across'

insights.claim: The RAD-BAARG discovery and imagery are credited to Hota et al. (2026) and the RAD@home Collaboratory, indicating the finding is tied to a citizen-science radio astronomy collaboration rather than a conventional professional survey.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Names the collaboration behind the find, useful for tracking follow-on publications on RAD-BAARG.
insights.locator: image credit, 'Credit: Hota et al. (2026) and the RAD@home Collaboratory'
